You can see from the table above that before the Pandemic, NIKE is not really a growth stock any more. Its revenues and gross profits were growing at single digit and EPS hovered between $1.17 and $2.51.

Between 2020 and 2022, Nike’s revenue rose from $37.4 billion to $46.7 billion, and then to $51.2 billion by 2023. Net income peaked at over $6 billion and EPS peaked at $3.75.

This period coincided with an extraordinary surge in demand for athletic wear, fueled by lockdowns, stimulus checks, and a shift toward casual clothing as people work from home. Nike’s stock, effectively on “steroids” during this time, peaked at around $170, trading at roughly 45× earnings—an unmistakable sign that Mr. market was pricing the temporary conditions as if they were permanent.

At the same time, Nike accelerated its direct-to-consumer strategy. By cutting out wholesalers and pushing sales through its own digital channels, it expanded margins and reinforced the perception that it was becoming a more efficient, higher-quality and growth business.

But those gains apparently proved to be more transient than sustainable. Revenue has since declined from $51.3 billion in 2024 to approximately $46.5 billion today. Net income has fallen from $5.7 billion to $2.5 billion. Gross profit has declined by over 12% year-over-year in the most recent period. In other words, a substantial portion of the pandemic-era gains has reversed.

What was priced as structural improvement of its business turned out primarily a function of temporary demand and favorable conditions.

What Now?

The recent underperformance of both NIKE's business and stock has not resulted from a cyclical wobble, as DECK and ONON have both been growing at double digits during the same time period. If you look at the above table, especially the YOY growth rates, you can tell that this is a business that has stopped growing and has seen meaningful earnings erosion. Furthermore, it is clearly not cutting its SG&A enough in the face of revenue decline.

However, NIKE's stock valuation has not yet fully adjusted to its slower business growth (if there is any ongoing growth at all), or its subpar profitability profile despite the fact that it had declined from its peak level around $170s to low $50s now, essentially where the stock traded in 2015. Why? it still trades at ~30× earnings and over 20× EBITDA.

That is the first and most important disconnect between the stock price and lackluster business fundamentals, unless Mr. Market is essentially betting on a quick and meaningful turnaround of its business.

What does that turnaround look like? and how likely can NIKE pull it out? What must happen to support Nike’s current valuation and then a modest amount of appreciation of its stock?

At the current 30× earnings, even assuming a normalized multiple of 20×, Nike would need to grow earnings materially just to prevent multiple compression from eroding shareholder returns.

If earnings were to remain flat at $2.5 billion, a re-rating from 30× to 20× alone would imply a one-third decline in equity value. It is psychologically difficult to accept that given the stock price has declined so much already from its $170s level to today's $50s. However, over the long term, fourth-grade math will trump human psychology.

On the other side of the coin, to sustain its current valuation (let's put aside any material stock price appreciation first), Nike would need to grow back earnings toward its prior peak of ~$5–6 billion, to >$4 billion (note its current market cap is ~$75 billion). That would require not only a recovery in revenue, but probably also a significant expansion in margins back toward double digits.

That is a tall order for a company of this scale that is operating in an increasingly competitive market. The apparently bloated workforce at NIKE creates a challenge to turn around such a large ship. The current macro-economic conditions, particularly consumer sentiments in the U.S. and elsewhere probably will not help NIKE in this endeavor, either.

I am not saying it is impossible, but it is a tall order for the company. At least based upon the 10-year data table above, I have not seen meaningful signs of a turnaround yet.

My overall prediction, if I have to venture one here, is that NIKE's stock will continue to hover around ~$40-$75 for the foreseeable future, depending on its own business performance and market sentiments. This is consistent with its 10-year EPS ranges between $1.17 and $3.75 above, limited growth potential and low profitability.

Conclusion

Capital should be allocated to businesses that are growing faster and/or more profitable than its peers, and are priced fairly relative to its probable potential to grow and earn profits to compensate for risks.

Nike is a large, mature company with declining earnings, subpar margins relative to peers, yet its stock is traded at a higher valuation that apparently assumes a degree of recovery that we can only hope for.

As stated in this previous article of mine covering primarily DECK,

I no longer wish to bet on such a turnaround [of NIKE] (or a turnaround of large-cap companies in general).

The reason is simple: whether lifting such a large ship can be done is far more difficult than an average individual investor can intelligently assess. Equally importantly, NIKE's sheer size of $90 billion market cap works against it, particularly for individual investors seeking nimble small- or mid-cap opportunities capable of delivering five- or ten-fold returns in the next 5-10 years. How can NIKE sell that many more sneakers to the world?

For the above reasons, I rate NIKE a SELL even though NIKE's market cap has declined further down to ~$75B now.

data.title: CAVA: Expansion Plans Justify Valuation
data.description:
In late 2025, I stated that Cava Group, Inc. (CAVA) was a “Buy.” I thought this founder-led company was outperforming similar fast casual peers like Chipotle (CMG) and Sweetgreens (SG) while creating new initiatives that I thought would position the business well in the long run.

Since that article, CAVA is up over 25% while the S&P 500 is down nearly 7%. To many, these types of quick corrections may lead investors to again believe this stock is overvalued.

I remain bullish on this business and I’m a believer in the long-term prospects of this company. Let’s dive into the most recent quarterly results as I share my perspective on this growth story.

Aggressive Expansion Plans

CAVA remains on track with their aggressive expansion plans as the company plans to open between 74-76 new locations in fiscal year 2026 with the ultimate goal of getting to 1,000 restaurants by 2032.

In 2026, CAVA will look to add stores in many Midwest cities including Cincinnati, Minneapolis, St. Louis, and Columbus. Over seven years (assuming the goal is hit at the end of 2032), CAVA would need to grow their store count by 12.5% annually. In 2025, CAVA opened 72, bringing their total store count to 439, which is nearly a 20% increase. I'd say that's impressive considering many competitors such as Sweetgreen have struggled to increase traffic and deliver a profitable bottom line (which I noted in another article), meanwhile CAVA has managed increase store count while growing revenue and maintaining positive same-store sales.

While 1,000 may sound like a big number when you compare that to the 3,938 restaurants Chipotle has in the United States you can see CAVA has a large amount of white space.

To support these expansion plans, management noted a few key initiatives, one of which I mentioned in my prior article was “Flavor Your Feature.” This is the company’s initiative to build out the company’s new generation of leaders. The company recently hired Doug Thompson to be the company’s Chief Operations Officer and he'll be responsible for helping to execute this initiative. One of the first steps within this initiative is adding more assistant general managers (AGMs). While it’s early days, CEO Bret Schulman noted the positives from AGMs on the company’s latest earnings call stating:

While still early, we're seeing encouraging signs that this approach is having a positive impact. Restaurants with AGM coverage are outperforming those without as AGMs provide additional leadership support during peak dinner and weekend shifts, helping to strengthen execution across every shift, develop future team members and create more sustainable restaurant teams.

The company’s management team also noted numerous technology enhancements including new kitchen display screen systems (KDS) and TurboChef ovens which will help ensure quality and consistency in offerings. Also, these will be a key aspect of future offerings such as the company’s pomegranate-glazed salmon which will be launching soon.

Lastly, in an effort to connect with guests and keep them coming back associated with the company’s new tiered loyalty program is “Oasis” which is the company’s invite-only tier. Oasis will give CAVA’s most loyal customers more perks, earning opportunities as well as exclusive merchandise.

While these all seem to be excellent plans to embolden the company’s continued expansion plans, risks certainly remain which I’ll discuss next.

Risks

CAVA lists numerous items that might impact the business going forward. I’m going to touch on a few risks noted in the company’s Risk Factors section in their most recent10K filing.

As I noted in an article about a similar growth restaurant company in Dutch Bros, aggressive expansion plans certainly increase operational risks. CAVA could have difficulties finding suitable real estate and securing financing. Then there’s the risk of hiring appropriately but hopefully that risk will be mitigated with the Flavor Your Feature initiative.

Competition remains a top risk , especially in this type of macro environment where consumers are being far more selective as the costs of dining out outpace the costs of eating food at home according to data from McKinsey. Further McKinsey data goes on to say consumers are making less frequent visits and are more sensitive to price increases. One positive for CAVA is that compared to other fast casual chains, the company has been less reliant on menu increases to drive revenue growth.

Lastly, I’d argue brand reputation damage is a risk for the business, especially if there’s an issue with the food. One notable example is the E. Coli issue Chipotle experienced years ago. Chipotle managed to turn things around but in today’s very competitive environment having such issues would certainly hurt the company’s reputation and could dampen the business’s expansion plans.

Financials

Before diving into the quarterly reports let’s zoom out and see how well CAVA has performed on an annual basis over the last few years.

As you can see below operating expenses have increased each year but revenue growth has outpaced these costs resulting in an increase to income from operations as well as an increase in income before taxes:

In fiscal year 2025, revenue grew by 22.5%. Same store sales increased by 4% and the company opened 72 new stores bringing the total number of restaurants to 439. You can see net income decreased in 2025 but that was due to a net benefit from the release of the valuation allowance against deferred tax assets. Excluding that amount, the comparable adjusted net income for fiscal year 2024 would be $50.2, which would result in a 26.9% increase in 2025.

CAVA shareholders can appreciate that dilution was very minimal in 2025 compared to 2024.

In Q4 2025, CAVA generated revenue of roughly $273 million which is a 21.2% increase compared to Q4 2024. Same sale growth for the quarter was nearly flat at 0.5%. CAVA’s restaurant level profit was $58.3 million for the quarter, which is a 15.7% increase compared to the prior year fourth quarter. Restaurant-level profit margin was 21.4%. The company opened 24 new restaurants in the quarter.

CAVA provided the following guidance for fiscal year 2026:

CAVA still has a solid balance sheet as you can see below with enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities and a shrinking accumulated deficit balance:

Valuation

CAVA has a valuation grade of a “F” at Seeking Alpha:

When looking at these metrics, whichever way you choose to value CAVA it’s hard not to argue it’s not expensive. As CAVA is still in its growth phase, I continue to believe the best valuation metric for this business is EV/Sales or Price-to-Sales. While CAVA's forward P/S is much higher than the sector median, it's still significantly lower than compared to this time last year:

Now let's look at CAVA's valuation from different angle having some assumptions about their expansion plans. Let’s assume CAVA hits their 1,000 store goal by 2032 and they’re able to continue to hold 4% same-store sales growth, which I think is conservative considering 2025 was a rough year for restaurants. Let’s also assume CAVA is able to scale and have some EBITDA margin expansion and let’s assume new stores open at higher AUVs (let’s say $3M plus). Using those inputs you’d get an implied share price of $180 as you can see in my model below:

In this model, CAVA would end up trading at 35X EBITDA. If CAVA kept trading at a premium like 45X or 55X EBITDA in 2032, then like a Chipotle, that’d certainly be a more compelling case. Still, with some base (if not conservative assumptions) I still think the case for CAVA is compelling.

No doubt CAVA's valuation is still expensive compared to established industry peers, I believe a premium is warranted in CAVA’s case as analysts expect CAVA to continue to grow revenue by over 20% annually over the next several years.

Conclusion

CAVA has rebounded since my prior article and it’s certainly understandable to say the company appears overvalued. Yet, if you’re like a long-term investor like I am then I think it’s easier to justify today’s prices given the company’s expansion plans.

Compared to industry peers, CAVA has proven they’re able to increase same stores sales even if a difficult macro setting and I believe they are making appropriate plans like their technology enhancements and AGM initiative to set themselves up in a winning position for years to come.

I’d urge investors to take the long view and assess the stock for it’s potential because if management can execute on their 1,000 store rollout by 2032, I’d say investors along for the ride will be quite pleased with their returns.
